title: Financial Ratios & Reading the Numbers Like a CFO
subhead: The module that separates a bookkeeper from an accountant. Producing the numbers is bookkeeping — interpreting them is accounting. Eight ratios, three months of Skarion data, and the narrative the numbers are telling you.
hook: "What does this P&L tell you?" Every controller, every CFO, and every interviewer asks this. The bookkeeper prints the statement. The accountant reads it. This module is the difference.

chunks.title: The 8 Ratios Every Accountant Must Know Cold
chunks.intro: A controller doesn't just close the books — they read them. These 8 ratios are how senior accountants immediately know whether a month was actually good or just looks good on the surface.
chunks.transcript:
0:00 — A controller doesn't just close the books. They read them. These 8 ratios are how senior accountants immediately know whether a month was actually good or just looks good on the surface.

0:30 — The 8, with Skarion's January numbers: Gross Profit Margin (Revenue − COGS) ÷ Revenue = ($480K − $140K) ÷ $480K = 70.8% — how profitable the core service is before overhead. Operating Profit Margin = Operating Income ÷ Revenue = $242K ÷ $480K = 50.4% — after all operating costs. Current Ratio = Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities = $1.6M ÷ $340K = 4.7x — can the company pay short-term bills? >2x is healthy. Quick Ratio = (Cash + AR) ÷ Current Liabilities = ($336K + $997K) ÷ $340K = 3.9x — more conservative, excludes prepaids/inventory.

1:30 — DSO = AR ÷ (Revenue ÷ 365 days) = $997K ÷ ($4.8M ÷ 365) = 75.8 days — how long to collect; >60 flags a cash risk. DPO = AP ÷ (COGS ÷ 365) = $280K ÷ ($1.68M ÷ 365) = 60.8 days — how long before you pay vendors. Debt-to-Equity = Total Liabilities ÷ Total Equity = $820K ÷ $2.1M = 0.39x — leverage; <1x is conservative. Return on Assets = Net Income ÷ Total Assets = $242K ÷ $4.02M = 6.0% — how efficiently assets generate profit.

2:30 — Here is the real skill: knowing when a ratio changes and why. Gross margin dropped from 70.8% to 65.2% between January and February. That tells you COGS went up faster than revenue — before you even look at the detail. The ratio is the canary; the detail is the mine.

4:00 — In the lab, you'll calculate all 8 ratios from Skarion's Q1 data and write one sentence of interpretation for each. That sentence is the start of your controller's commentary.

chunks.title: The 8 Ratios — Formulas, Skarion January, and What Each Tells You
chunks.bodyHtml:
Skarion's January financials: Revenue $480,000 · COGS $140,000 · Operating Expenses $98,000 · Net Income $242,000. Current Assets ≈ $1.6M (Cash $336,250 + AR $997,700 + prepaids), Current Liabilities ≈ $340K, Total Liabilities ≈ $820K, Total Equity ≈ $2.1M, Total Assets ≈ $4.02M, annualized revenue ≈ $4.8M, annualized COGS ≈ $1.68M.

RatioFormulaSkarion JanWhat it tells you
Gross Profit Margin(Revenue − COGS) ÷ Revenue70.8%Profitability of the core service before overhead
Operating Profit MarginOperating Income ÷ Revenue50.4%Profitability after all operating costs
Current RatioCurrent Assets ÷ Current Liabilities4.7xCan the company pay short-term bills? >2x healthy
Quick Ratio(Cash + AR) ÷ Current Liabilities3.9xConservative liquidity (excludes prepaids/inventory)
DSOAR ÷ (Revenue ÷ 365)75.8 daysHow long to collect; >60 flags a cash risk
DPOAP ÷ (COGS ÷ 365)60.8 daysHow long before you pay vendors
Debt-to-EquityTotal Liabilities ÷ Total Equity0.39xFinancial leverage; <1x is conservative
Return on AssetsNet Income ÷ Total Assets6.0%How efficiently assets generate profit

callout.html: Read the ratio like a sentence. 70.8% gross margin + 75.8 day DSO + 0.39x leverage reads as: "The core business is highly profitable, but we're slow to collect, and we run almost no debt." One sentence that a CFO would say is worth more than ten decimal places of calculation.

chunks.title: Ratio Dashboard — Calculate All 8 From Skarion January
chunks.scenarioHtml: Calculate Skarion's 8 financial ratios live in the spreadsheet. The P&L and Balance Sheet figures (Revenue, COGS, OpEx, Cash, AR, AP, Total Liabilities, Total Equity, Total Assets) are pre-filled and locked in column A. Enter each ratio formula result in column C. The lab validates when all 8 match the target values within 0.1.
chunks.successMessage: ✅ All 8 ratios within tolerance — ratio analysis complete. You're reading financials like a CFO.
chunks.errorMessage: ❌ One or more ratios are off. Check the formulas in the Chunk 91 table — e.g. Gross Margin = (480−140)/480 × 100 = 70.8; DSO = 997700/(4800000/365) = 75.8.

chunks.title: What the Numbers Are Trying to Tell You — Reading the Story
chunks.bodyHtml:
The hardest skill: interpreting a set of ratios as a narrative. A single ratio is a number. Three ratios together tell a story.

Scenario A — Revenue up 15%, gross margin down 8%, DSO up from 45 to 72 days. What's happening? Revenue growth is hollow — COGS is rising faster, and clients are paying slower. Cash is about to tighten despite the revenue number looking good. The wrong reads: "business is growing well" (ignores the margin) or "costs are under control" (ignores DSO).

Scenario B — Net income up 20%, current ratio down from 3.2x to 1.4x, DPO down from 45 to 22 days. Profitability improved but liquidity is deteriorating — the company is paying vendors faster than it's collecting, and cash is being squeezed. Profit without liquidity is how profitable companies miss payroll.

Scenario C — Gross margin stable, operating margin declining, return on assets declining. The core business is fine; operating expenses are growing — probably headcount or SG&A — eating into profits the core business is generating. The fix is overhead, not pricing.

chunks.title: Reading Skarion's Q1 Ratios — Spot the Real Story
chunks.intro: Apply ratio interpretation to Skarion's Q1 data. Don't just calculate — say what the number means.

questions.q: Skarion's Q1 ratio dashboard shows January gross margin 70.8%, February 65.2%, March 62.1%. What is the most likely root cause?
options: Offshore drafting rates rose faster than Skarion raised its client prices
Skarion took on too much debt
questions.explanation: Margin compression at a services firm with stable revenue usually means input costs (offshore drafting) rose faster than pricing. DSO would show a collection story; debt/rent land in OpEx, not Gross Margin.

questions.q: Which Q1 month had the worst DSO, and what does it flag?
options: January (75.8 days) — collections trending slow, watch TeleCom Builders
March, because net income was highest
questions.explanation: January carries the 75.8-day DSO (the TeleCom 90+ bucket before the settlement arrives). Slowing DSO is a leading cash-flow indicator — it flags itself before cash actually dips.

questions.q: Skarion's current ratio is 4.7x and quick ratio is 3.9x. What concern, if any, should the controller raise?
options: None on liquidity — both ratios are strong; the concern is DSO, not solvency
The current ratio is dangerously low
The quick ratio being lower than current ratio means fraud
questions.explanation: Strong liquidity both ways. The flagged concern is the 75.8-day DSO tying up cash in slow AR — liquidity is fine today, but watch the collections trend.

questions.q: Net income is strong and rising, but cash is flat. Which two ratios together explain the disconnect?
options: High DSO (cash stuck in AR) + low DPO (paying vendors fast)
High gross margin + low operating margin
High current ratio + high quick ratio
questions.explanation: Profit → Cash gap is almost always the working-capital combination: slow collections (high DSO) + fast payments (low DPO). The Cash Flow Statement's operating section makes this exact gap visible.
